title: Save, Spend, Share
developmental_frame: At 9–10, money is becoming a bit more real, and children can genuinely grasp the three-jar idea — some to save, some to spend, some to share — if it's concrete and the amounts are tiny. Waiting (delayed gratification) is hard at this age, so a saving goal must be short-term and visible. Having made and offered something in Q1, they now meet the natural next question: what do you do with what you get?
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is feeling genuinely in charge of your own small money — making a plan on purpose instead of spending it all the moment you get it. The reward is the grown-up feeling of having decided, and watching a small saving goal actually get closer.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child is trusted to manage a small, real amount of their own money and make real choices about it. Being in charge — genuinely deciding save vs spend vs share, and watching a goal jar fill — is deeply motivating at this age. The trust itself is the driver; nobody has to force a plan when the money and the choice are really theirs.

artifact: A three-jar (save / spend / share) plan for a small real amount of money, with a chosen saving goal and at least one real money choice recorded.
success_criteria: The child split a small real amount into save, spend, and share.
They chose a real short-term saving goal and can name it.
They can explain one money choice they made and why.

lessons.title: Money can do three jobs
lessons.concept: Money isn't just for spending right now. It can do three jobs: **save** (keep for something bigger later), **spend** (enjoy now), and **share** (give to help someone). Smart money people use all three.
lessons.real_example: Get RM6? You might put RM2 to save, RM2 to spend, RM2 to share. Three little jars, three jobs — and you decide how much goes where.
lessons.apply_task: Set up three jars (or cups) labelled Save, Spend, Share. They're ready for your money.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which jar do you think will be hardest to put money in? For most people it's Save — because waiting is hard.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your three jars to a parent and talk about what each one is for.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain the three jobs of money to a younger child using the jars.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is about your real money and real jars you can touch — much more powerful than anything on a screen. Set them up for real.
success_criteria: Three labelled jars are set up.

lessons.title: Pick a save goal
lessons.concept: Saving is much easier when you're saving *for* something. Pick one thing you really want that costs a bit more than you have now. That's your goal — the reason the Save jar is worth it.
lessons.real_example: Wanting a RM15 toy when you get RM3 a week means saving over a few weeks. But now every coin in the Save jar is exciting, because it's getting you closer.
lessons.apply_task: Choose one real thing you want to save for. Write it on your Save jar and how much it costs.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is your goal something you'll still want in a few weeks? Good saving goals are things you really care about.
lessons.feedback_task: Tell a parent your save goal and check it's a realistic thing to save for.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a sibling pick a save goal worth waiting for.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Your goal is about what *you* really want — that's yours alone to choose. No computer knows what would make you happy.
success_criteria: A real, specific saving goal is chosen and its cost noted.

lessons.title: Choose on purpose
lessons.concept: When you get some money, decide *on purpose* how to split it across your three jars — instead of spending it all at once. This is you being the boss of your money, not the other way round.
lessons.real_example: You get RM6. You *could* spend it all on sweets. Or you choose: RM3 to save for the toy, RM2 to spend now, RM1 to share. That's a plan you made, not an accident.
lessons.apply_task: Take a small real amount of money and split it across your three jars on purpose. Write down how you split it.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was it tempting to put it all in Spend? How did it feel to save some on purpose?
lessons.feedback_task: Show a parent your split and explain why you chose it. Ask what they think.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone how you split your money on purpose across the three jars.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This choice is yours to make — being the boss of your own money is the whole point, and that can't be handed to a computer.
success_criteria: A real amount was split across the three jars with the split recorded.

lessons.title: Watch it grow
lessons.concept: Keep adding to your Save jar and watch it get closer to your goal. Seeing it grow — and finally reaching the goal — teaches the best money lesson of all: waiting a little can get you something better.
lessons.real_example: Week by week the Save jar fills. The day it finally holds enough for the toy feels amazing — better than spending it on small stuff would have. That feeling is the reward for patience.
lessons.apply_task: Track your Save jar over the next few weeks. Mark how close you are to your goal each week.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How does it feel to watch your savings grow toward something you want? Was the waiting worth it?
lessons.feedback_task: Show a parent your progress tracker. Celebrate together when you hit the goal.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone the story of saving up and reaching your goal — and how waiting paid off.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Watching real coins fill a real jar toward a real goal is the lesson here — no app needed. The patience is yours to build.
success_criteria: The child tracked their savings toward the goal over time.
They can explain what saving on purpose taught them.
